skill.name: bevy-ecs-expert
skill.description: Learn Bevy ECS architecture in Rust, including components, systems, resources, queries, bundles, and scheduling.
skill.summary: Build clearer and faster Bevy ECS game logic with Rust patterns for systems, queries, resources, and scheduling.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 27 external command patterns and two weak cryptography patterns in SKILL.md. Manual review found these are false positives from Markdown and Rust examples: Bevy's commands.spawn creates ECS entities, and the flagged prose is not cryptographic code. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, network access, filesystem access, or executable script behavior was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Bevy Entity Spawn API
low_findings.description: The high external command finding points to commands.spawn in a Rust Bevy example. This API creates an ECS entity and does not start an operating system process or shell command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding Rust example defines setup with Commands and PlayerBundle. The call is clearly Bevy ECS entity spawning, not child process execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks and Rust Examples
low_findings.description: The medium external command findings are Markdown inline code and fenced Rust examples for Component, Query, Resource, With, Without, Bundle, RefCell, and related Bevy concepts. They do not execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is documentation and Rust code snippets inside Markdown. No shell interpreter, subprocess API, or dynamic command construction appears in these locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings match ordinary skill metadata and usage prose about ECS and designing systems. No cryptographic primitive, hashing code, or encryption API is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both cited lines are prose or metadata, not executable code. The matches appear to be substring collisions rather than actual DES, MD5, or other weak cryptographic use.

content.value_statement: Bevy ECS code can become hard to structure when systems, queries, resources, and scheduling rules interact. This skill gives practical Rust guidance for building clear, parallel-friendly game logic.
actual_capabilities: Explains Bevy ECS concepts such as components, systems, resources, bundles, and queries.
Shows Rust examples for component definitions, query filters, entity spawning, and system scheduling.
Guides decisions about Res, ResMut, Query filters, and execution order.
Helps refactor object-oriented gameplay logic into data-oriented ECS patterns.
Provides troubleshooting guidance for common ECS borrow and scheduling conflicts.
limitations: Does not include executable scripts, project scaffolding, or automated code generation.
Examples may need updates for specific Bevy versions and project conventions.
Does not cover rendering, physics, networking, or asset pipelines in depth.
Cannot benchmark or verify performance without access to the target project.

use_cases.title: Structure Gameplay Systems
use_cases.description: Plan components, systems, resources, and bundles for new Bevy gameplay features.

use_cases.title: Learn ECS Patterns
use_cases.description: Understand how Bevy uses data-oriented Rust patterns for game state and behavior.

use_cases.title: Review ECS Architecture
use_cases.description: Evaluate system ordering, query design, and resource access before a gameplay refactor.

prompt_templates.title: Explain Bevy ECS Basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how Bevy ECS uses components, systems, resources, and queries. Keep the explanation focused on practical game development.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Component Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design Bevy ECS components and resources for this gameplay feature: [describe feature]. Include query and bundle recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Query and Schedule
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Bevy ECS system design for query efficiency and parallel scheduling risks: [describe systems]. Suggest improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Review Parallel ECS Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Act as a Bevy ECS reviewer for this architecture: [describe components, resources, and systems]. Identify borrow conflicts, ordering issues, and data layout problems.

output_examples.input: I need enemy AI that ignores dead entities and moves active enemies toward the player.
output: Define separate marker components for active enemies, dead enemies, and the player target.
Use query filters so the enemy system skips dead entities before iteration.
Keep movement data in components and store shared difficulty or tuning values in resources.

output_examples.input: My Bevy systems conflict when two systems update the same component.
output: Check whether both systems need mutable access in the same schedule.
Split read-only logic from write logic where possible.
Use explicit ordering only when the data dependency is real.

output_examples.input: I am moving an object-oriented game object into Bevy ECS.
output: Separate identity, state, movement, and behavior flags into small components.
Move shared state into resources only when it is truly global.
Use systems to express behavior instead of storing behavior inside components.

best_practices: Keep components as simple data and put behavior in systems.
Use query filters to reduce unnecessary iteration and clarify intent.
Prefer read-only resource access when mutation is not required.
anti_patterns: Do not store heavy gameplay logic inside components.
Do not use interior mutability to bypass ECS borrowing rules.
Do not force system ordering unless there is a clear data dependency.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps design and review Bevy ECS code for components, systems, resources, queries, bundles, and scheduling.

faq.question: Does it create a complete Bevy project?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples, but it does not scaffold or run a Bevy project.

faq.question: Is it useful for new Rust developers?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains ECS concepts with practical Rust examples, but basic Rust knowledge is still helpful.

faq.question: Can it help with performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It highlights query filters, data layout, resource access, and scheduling choices that can affect performance.

faq.question: Does it cover all Bevy engine systems?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on ECS architecture and does not deeply cover rendering, physics, networking, or assets.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows that involve Bevy ECS design.
